topics.title: Adolescent Development And Puberty
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescence is the developmental transition between childhood and adulthood, typically 10-19 years (WHO), characterised by rapid physical, cognitive, psychosocial and sexual maturation driven by puberty. Puberty is the biological process of sexual maturation (adrenarche then gonadarche) producing adult reproductive capacity; it typically begins 8-13 in girls and 9-14 in boys, lasting 2-5 years. Adolescents require a distinct clinical approach because biological, psychological and social development are asynchronous, risk-taking peaks mid-adolescence, and chronic disease/adherence/transition issues cluster in this age group.

Core Clinical Content

WHO definition

10-19 years (early 10-14, mid 15-17, late 18-19)

Puberty timing

Girls 8-13, Boys 9-14 (2-5 years duration)

Tanner staging

5 stages for breast (B), pubic hair (PH), genital (G), testicular volume (ml)

Adrenarche

Adrenal androgen rise ~6-8 yrs; pubarche (pubic hair) often first sign

Gonadarche

HPG axis activation; first sign is testicular enlargement >=4 mL in boys, thelarche in girls

Growth spurt

Girls peak height velocity 11-12 yrs; boys 13-14 yrs; ~20-25% final height

Brain development

Prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s; limbic system earlier -> risk-taking

Psychosocial tasks

Identity, autonomy, peer relationships, sexual identity, education/vocation

Identity vs role confusion

Erikson: 5th stage of psychosocial development

Risk behaviour window

Mid-adolescence (15-17 yrs) is the peak for risk-taking, novelty-seeking

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

Puberty timingGirls 8-13, Boys 9-14 (2-5 years duration)

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Adolescent development and puberty.

Adolescence is the developmental transition between childhood and adulthood, typically 10-19 years (WHO), characterised by rapid physical, cognitive, psychosocial and sexual maturation driven by puberty. Puberty is the biological process of sexual maturation (adrenarche then gonadarche) producing adult reproductive capacity; it typically begins 8-13 in girls and 9-14 in boys, lasting 2-5 years. Adolescents require a distinct clinical approach because biological, psychological and social development are asynchronous, risk-taking peaks mid-adolescence, and chronic disease/adherence/transition issues cluster in this age group.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adolescent development and puberty?

WHO definition: 10-19 years (early 10-14, mid 15-17, late 18-19). Puberty timing: Girls 8-13, Boys 9-14 (2-5 years duration). Tanner staging: 5 stages for breast (B), pubic hair (PH), genital (G), testicular volume (ml). See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adolescent development and puberty recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Adrenarche - Adrenal androgen rise ~6-8 yrs; pubarche (pubic hair) often first sign; Gonadarche - HPG axis activation; first sign is testicular enlargement >=4 mL in boys, thelarche in girls. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adolescent development and puberty?

Investigations include: Growth spurt - Girls peak height velocity 11-12 yrs; boys 13-14 yrs; ~20-25% final height; Brain development - Prefrontal cortex matures into mid-20s; limbic system earlier -> risk-taking. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adolescent development and puberty?

First-line management: Psychosocial tasks - Identity, autonomy, peer relationships, sexual identity, education/vocation; Identity vs role confusion - Erikson: 5th stage of psychosocial development. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adolescent development and puberty?

Red flags and complications: Risk behaviour window - Mid-adolescence (15-17 yrs) is the peak for risk-taking, novelty-seeking. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adolescent development and puberty - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Communicating With Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Effective communication with adolescents is the cornerstone of adolescent medicine, requiring a confidential, non-judgemental, developmentally-appropriate approach. The HEADSS or HEEADSSS psychosocial interview is the validated framework (Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety; expanded to include Eating and Sleep). Effective engagement requires time alone with the adolescent (without parents), establishing confidentiality, using open questions, avoiding jargon, and being aware of the impact of parental presence on disclosure. Communication is consistently cited as the most important adolescent clinical skill in FCPS/MRCP vivas.

Core Clinical Content

Time alone

Always offer to see adolescent without parents for at least part of the consultation

Confidentiality

Explain limits upfront: confidential unless you or someone else is at risk

HEADSS framework

Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression

HEEADSSS expansion

Adds Eating, Sleep, Safety, Suicide (modern 3.0 adds Screen time)

Open questions

Avoid leading questions; tell me about, how do you feel about

Engagement order

Start with less sensitive (school, hobbies), build to more personal (sex, drugs)

Triadic consultation

Adolescent + parent + clinician; balance parental concerns with adolescent voice

Body language

Eye level, no barriers, unhurried, no laptop screen blocking

3-month rule

Teens often cannot plan beyond 3 months - use this for appointments and contraception

Termination

Summarise, agree plan, clarify follow-up; do not break trust at the end

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Time aloneAlways offer to see adolescent without parents for at least part of the consultation
ConfidentialityExplain limits upfront: confidential unless you or someone else is at risk
HEADSS frameworkHome, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression
HEEADSSS expansionAdds Eating, Sleep, Safety, Suicide (modern 3.0 adds Screen time)
Open questionsAvoid leading questions; tell me about, how do you feel about

Triadic consultationAdolescent + parent + clinician; balance parental concerns with adolescent voice
Body languageEye level, no barriers, unhurried, no laptop screen blocking
3-month ruleTeens often cannot plan beyond 3 months - use this for appointments and contraception
TerminationSummarise, agree plan, clarify follow-up; do not break trust at the end

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Communicating with adolescents.

Effective communication with adolescents is the cornerstone of adolescent medicine, requiring a confidential, non-judgemental, developmentally-appropriate approach. The HEADSS or HEEADSSS psychosocial interview is the validated framework (Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety; expanded to include Eating and Sleep). Effective engagement requires time alone with the adolescent (without parents), establishing confidentiality, using open questions, avoiding jargon, and being aware of the impact of parental presence on disclosure. Communication is consistently cited as the most important adolescent clinical skill in FCPS/MRCP vivas.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Communicating with adolescents?

Time alone: Always offer to see adolescent without parents for at least part of the consultation. Confidentiality: Explain limits upfront: confidential unless you or someone else is at risk. HEADSS framework: Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Communicating with adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: HEEADSSS expansion - Adds Eating, Sleep, Safety, Suicide (modern 3.0 adds Screen time); Open questions - Avoid leading questions; tell me about, how do you feel about. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Communicating with adolescents?

Investigations include: Engagement order - Start with less sensitive (school, hobbies), build to more personal (sex, drugs); Triadic consultation - Adolescent + parent + clinician; balance parental concerns with adolescent voice. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Communicating with adolescents?

First-line management: Body language - Eye level, no barriers, unhurried, no laptop screen blocking; 3-month rule - Teens often cannot plan beyond 3 months - use this for appointments and contraception. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Communicating with adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Termination - Summarise, agree plan, clarify follow-up; do not break trust at the end. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Communicating with adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Psychosocial Assessment Heeadsss
topics.html:
Definition

HEEADSSS is the modern, evidence-based psychosocial assessment for adolescents, expanding the older HEADSS framework. It is a structured, non-linear interview that should be offered to all adolescents regardless of presenting complaint, with the order adapted to the individual. The 3.0 version adds Screen time reflecting the digital era. The 7-8-minute interview identifies risk behaviours, mental health concerns, and resilience factors. It is the recommended screening tool in FCPS/MRCP adolescent clinical skills stations and is the basis of the HEEADSSS-SSHADESS strength-based variations.

Core Clinical Content

H - Home

Who lives with you? Any recent changes? Safety at home?

E - Education/Employment

School year, performance, attendance, work hours (<20 hr/wk protective)

E - Eating

Diet, body image, weight concerns, screen for eating disorder

A - Activities

Hobbies, sport, screen time, social media, peer group

D - Drugs

Cigarettes, alcohol, cannabis, vaping, prescription, illicit drugs

S - Sexuality

Gender identity, sexual activity, contraception, STIs, partners

S - Suicide/Self-harm

Mood, sleep, hopelessness, prior attempts, ideation

S - Safety

Abuse, bullying, seatbelt, weapons, online safety

Plus Screen time (3.0)

Hours/day, content, social media, gaming, sleep impact

Strengths/Resilience

Always end with strengths: what are you good at? protective factors

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
H - HomeWho lives with you? Any recent changes? Safety at home?
E - Education/EmploymentSchool year, performance, attendance, work hours (<20 hr/wk protective)
E - EatingDiet, body image, weight concerns, screen for eating disorder
A - ActivitiesHobbies, sport, screen time, social media, peer group
D - DrugsCigarettes, alcohol, cannabis, vaping, prescription, illicit drugs
S - SexualityGender identity, sexual activity, contraception, STIs, partners
S - Suicide/Self-harmMood, sleep, hopelessness, prior attempts, ideation
S - SafetyAbuse, bullying, seatbelt, weapons, online safety
Plus Screen time (3.0)Hours/day, content, social media, gaming, sleep impact
Strengths/ResilienceAlways end with strengths: what are you good at? protective factors

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS.

HEEADSSS is the modern, evidence-based psychosocial assessment for adolescents, expanding the older HEADSS framework. It is a structured, non-linear interview that should be offered to all adolescents regardless of presenting complaint, with the order adapted to the individual. The 3.0 version adds Screen time reflecting the digital era. The 7-8-minute interview identifies risk behaviours, mental health concerns, and resilience factors. It is the recommended screening tool in FCPS/MRCP adolescent clinical skills stations and is the basis of the HEEADSSS-SSHADESS strength-based variations.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS?

H - Home: Who lives with you? Any recent changes? Safety at home?. E - Education/Employment: School year, performance, attendance, work hours (<20 hr/wk protective). E - Eating: Diet, body image, weight concerns, screen for eating disorder. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: A - Activities - Hobbies, sport, screen time, social media, peer group; D - Drugs - Cigarettes, alcohol, cannabis, vaping, prescription, illicit drugs. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS?

Investigations include: S - Sexuality - Gender identity, sexual activity, contraception, STIs, partners; S - Suicide/Self-harm - Mood, sleep, hopelessness, prior attempts, ideation. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS?

First-line management: S - Safety - Abuse, bullying, seatbelt, weapons, online safety; Plus Screen time (3.0) - Hours/day, content, social media, gaming, sleep impact. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS?

Red flags and complications: Strengths/Resilience - Always end with strengths: what are you good at? protective factors. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Psychosocial assessment - HEEADSSS - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Risk Taking Behaviour In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents includes substance use, unsafe sexual activity, dangerous driving, violence, self-harm, and disordered eating. It peaks in mid-adolescence (15-17) and is driven by the asynchronous maturation of the socio-emotional (limbic) system, which is reward-sensitive, and the cognitive control (prefrontal) system, which matures into the mid-20s. Most risk-taking is exploratory and developmentally normal; persistent risk-taking or harm-promoting risk-taking signals distress. Anticipatory guidance, motivational interviewing, and harm-reduction are evidence-based approaches; punitive responses worsen outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Peak age

Mid-adolescence 15-17 years; declines by 20-25

Dual systems model

Limbic (reward) matures early; prefrontal (control) matures late

Sensation-seeking

Normal developmental drive; novelty and peer influence amplify it

Common risks

Alcohol, cannabis, nicotine/vaping, unsafe sex, dangerous driving, fighting, self-harm

Protective factors

Connected family, school engagement, <20 hr/wk work, religious/community ties

Risk factors

Adverse childhood events, mental illness, peer substance use, family disruption

Anticipatory guidance

Discuss before risk occurs; not as a single lecture but ongoing

Motivational interviewing

Open questions, reflective listening, elicit change talk

Harm reduction

Safer-use advice, naloxone for opioids, contraception access, designated driver

Red flags

Risk-taking + mood change, school decline, social withdrawal, sleep change

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Peak ageMid-adolescence 15-17 years; declines by 20-25
Dual systems modelLimbic (reward) matures early; prefrontal (control) matures late
Sensation-seekingNormal developmental drive; novelty and peer influence amplify it
Common risksAlcohol, cannabis, nicotine/vaping, unsafe sex, dangerous driving, fighting, self-harm
Protective factorsConnected family, school engagement, <20 hr/wk work, religious/community ties
Risk factorsAdverse childhood events, mental illness, peer substance use, family disruption
Anticipatory guidanceDiscuss before risk occurs; not as a single lecture but ongoing
Motivational interviewingOpen questions, reflective listening, elicit change talk
Harm reductionSafer-use advice, naloxone for opioids, contraception access, designated driver
Red flagsRisk-taking + mood change, school decline, social withdrawal, sleep change

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents.

Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents includes substance use, unsafe sexual activity, dangerous driving, violence, self-harm, and disordered eating. It peaks in mid-adolescence (15-17) and is driven by the asynchronous maturation of the socio-emotional (limbic) system, which is reward-sensitive, and the cognitive control (prefrontal) system, which matures into the mid-20s. Most risk-taking is exploratory and developmentally normal; persistent risk-taking or harm-promoting risk-taking signals distress. Anticipatory guidance, motivational interviewing, and harm-reduction are evidence-based approaches; punitive responses worsen outcomes.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents?

Peak age: Mid-adolescence 15-17 years; declines by 20-25. Dual systems model: Limbic (reward) matures early; prefrontal (control) matures late. Sensation-seeking: Normal developmental drive; novelty and peer influence amplify it. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Common risks - Alcohol, cannabis, nicotine/vaping, unsafe sex, dangerous driving, fighting, self-harm; Protective factors - Connected family, school engagement, <20 hr/wk work, religious/community ties. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents?

Investigations include: Risk factors - Adverse childhood events, mental illness, peer substance use, family disruption; Anticipatory guidance - Discuss before risk occurs; not as a single lecture but ongoing. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents?

First-line management: Motivational interviewing - Open questions, reflective listening, elicit change talk; Harm reduction - Safer-use advice, naloxone for opioids, contraception access, designated driver. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Red flags - Risk-taking + mood change, school decline, social withdrawal, sleep change. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Risk-taking behaviour in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Confidentiality In Adolescent Care
topics.html:
Definition

Confidentiality is the foundation of adolescent healthcare: 60-80% of adolescents would not disclose sensitive information (sex, drugs, mental health) without confidentiality assurance. The duty of confidentiality is not absolute: it can be broken to protect the young person (suicide risk, abuse) or others (serious harm to identifiable third party). GMC, BMA and Royal College of Paediatrics guidance confirm best practice. Confidentiality must be explained at the first contact, in language the adolescent understands, and the limits clearly stated.

Core Clinical Content

Disclosure rates

Up to 80% will not disclose sensitive issues if confidentiality not assured

Core principle

Adolescent interests best served by confidentiality (with limits explained)

Absolute vs relative

Not absolute: disclose if serious harm to young person or others

GMC guidance

Disclose if necessary to prevent serious harm; consult colleague first

Sexual activity

Under-13 sexual activity must be referred (safeguarding); 13-15 assessed case-by-case

Drug/alcohol use

Generally confidential; address acute medical issues first

Mental health/suicide

Confidential unless active suicidal intent with plan - then act to protect

Parents

Generally encouraged to involve parents, but adolescent may refuse if competent

Records

Confidential, with redactions in summaries; IT access controls

EHR for adolescents

Confidential flags, password protection, no automatic parental proxy access

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Disclosure ratesUp to 80% will not disclose sensitive issues if confidentiality not assured
Core principleAdolescent interests best served by confidentiality (with limits explained)
Absolute vs relativeNot absolute: disclose if serious harm to young person or others
GMC guidanceDisclose if necessary to prevent serious harm; consult colleague first
Sexual activityUnder-13 sexual activity must be referred (safeguarding); 13-15 assessed case-by-case
Drug/alcohol useGenerally confidential; address acute medical issues first
Mental health/suicideConfidential unless active suicidal intent with plan - then act to protect
ParentsGenerally encouraged to involve parents, but adolescent may refuse if competent
RecordsConfidential, with redactions in summaries; IT access controls
EHR for adolescentsConfidential flags, password protection, no automatic parental proxy access

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Confidentiality in adolescent care.

Confidentiality is the foundation of adolescent healthcare: 60-80% of adolescents would not disclose sensitive information (sex, drugs, mental health) without confidentiality assurance. The duty of confidentiality is not absolute: it can be broken to protect the young person (suicide risk, abuse) or others (serious harm to identifiable third party). GMC, BMA and Royal College of Paediatrics guidance confirm best practice. Confidentiality must be explained at the first contact, in language the adolescent understands, and the limits clearly stated.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Confidentiality in adolescent care?

Disclosure rates: Up to 80% will not disclose sensitive issues if confidentiality not assured. Core principle: Adolescent interests best served by confidentiality (with limits explained). Absolute vs relative: Not absolute: disclose if serious harm to young person or others. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Confidentiality in adolescent care recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: GMC guidance - Disclose if necessary to prevent serious harm; consult colleague first; Sexual activity - Under-13 sexual activity must be referred (safeguarding); 13-15 assessed case-by-case. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Confidentiality in adolescent care?

Investigations include: Drug/alcohol use - Generally confidential; address acute medical issues first; Mental health/suicide - Confidential unless active suicidal intent with plan - then act to protect. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Confidentiality in adolescent care?

First-line management: Parents - Generally encouraged to involve parents, but adolescent may refuse if competent; Records - Confidential, with redactions in summaries; IT access controls. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Confidentiality in adolescent care?

Red flags and complications: EHR for adolescents - Confidential flags, password protection, no automatic parental proxy access. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Confidentiality in adolescent care - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Consent In Adolescence Gillick And Fraser
topics.html:
Definition

Consent in adolescence balances the developing autonomy of the young person with their need for protection. The English legal test for under-16s is Gillick competence (capacity to understand the nature, purpose and risks of treatment), and Fraser guidelines (specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual-health advice without parental knowledge). The Mental Capacity Act 2005 (16+) and Family Law Reform Act 1969 (16-17 can consent as adults) apply. Competent minors can consent to treatment but cannot refuse life-saving treatment in the same way adults can (court authority required).

Core Clinical Content

Gillick competence

Under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions

Fraser guidelines

Specific: contraception, sexual health - used when no parental involvement

Test of competence

Understands nature/purpose, retains information, weighs up, communicates decision

16-17 year olds

Presumed capable (Mental Capacity Act 2005, Family Law Reform Act 1969)

Can consent but not refuse

Mature minor can consent; cannot refuse life-saving treatment (court)

Parental role

Should usually be involved; not required if adolescent is competent and refuses

Documentation

Record competence assessment: understands condition, options, risks, benefits

Court involvement

Disputes over refusal in competent minors -> Court of Protection / High Court

Looked-after children

Local authority shares parental responsibility; complex - seek legal advice

Emergency treatment

Can be given to under-16 if life-saving and parents not contactable

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Gillick competenceUnder-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
Fraser guidelinesSpecific: contraception, sexual health - used when no parental involvement
Test of competenceUnderstands nature/purpose, retains information, weighs up, communicates decision
16-17 year oldsPresumed capable (Mental Capacity Act 2005, Family Law Reform Act 1969)
Can consent but not refuseMature minor can consent; cannot refuse life-saving treatment (court)
Parental roleShould usually be involved; not required if adolescent is competent and refuses
DocumentationRecord competence assessment: understands condition, options, risks, benefits
Court involvementDisputes over refusal in competent minors -> Court of Protection / High Court
Looked-after childrenLocal authority shares parental responsibility; complex - seek legal advice
Emergency treatmentCan be given to under-16 if life-saving and parents not contactable

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser.

Consent in adolescence balances the developing autonomy of the young person with their need for protection. The English legal test for under-16s is Gillick competence (capacity to understand the nature, purpose and risks of treatment), and Fraser guidelines (specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual-health advice without parental knowledge). The Mental Capacity Act 2005 (16+) and Family Law Reform Act 1969 (16-17 can consent as adults) apply. Competent minors can consent to treatment but cannot refuse life-saving treatment in the same way adults can (court authority required).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser?

Gillick competence: Under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions. Fraser guidelines: Specific: contraception, sexual health - used when no parental involvement. Test of competence: Understands nature/purpose, retains information, weighs up, communicates decision. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: 16-17 year olds - Presumed capable (Mental Capacity Act 2005, Family Law Reform Act 1969); Can consent but not refuse - Mature minor can consent; cannot refuse life-saving treatment (court). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser?

Investigations include: Parental role - Should usually be involved; not required if adolescent is competent and refuses; Documentation - Record competence assessment: understands condition, options, risks, benefits. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser?

First-line management: Court involvement - Disputes over refusal in competent minors -> Court of Protection / High Court; Looked-after children - Local authority shares parental responsibility; complex - seek legal advice. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser?

Red flags and complications: Emergency treatment - Can be given to under-16 if life-saving and parents not contactable. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

Mind Map

```

Consent in adolescence - Gillick and Fraser
```

topics.title: Safeguarding And Child Protection In Adolescence
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescents are vulnerable to safeguarding concerns including physical, sexual and emotional abuse, neglect, exploitation (child sexual exploitation, county lines, online exploitation), radicalisation, and harm from outside the home. The Children Acts 1989/2004 require Local Authorities to safeguard children in need and protect those at risk of significant harm. The Working Together to Safeguard Children framework sets the multi-agency approach. Adolescent safeguarding is distinct from younger children - they are more likely to experience extra-familial harm, self-neglect, exploitation and mental health crises rather than neglect or physical abuse at home.

Core Clinical Content

Definition of harm

Significant harm = ill-treatment or impairment of health/developmental wellbeing

Types of abuse

Physical, sexual, emotional, neglect, exploitation (CSE, criminal, online)

Children Acts

1989/2004: duty to safeguard; Section 47 enquiry if risk of significant harm

Working Together

Multi-agency guidance; named professionals; LADO for allegations against staff

Section 47 enquiry

Local authority investigation of risk of significant harm

Child protection plan

If at continuing risk; CP conference, core group, plan review

Adolescent-specific

Extra-familial harm, exploitation, county lines, online harms, self-neglect

CSE (child sexual exploitation)

Under-18 in sexual activity in exchange for something; coercion key

County lines

Gangs recruiting children to carry drugs; exploitation and modern slavery

Information sharing

Fears of breaching data protection NOT a barrier to safeguarding

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Definition of harmSignificant harm = ill-treatment or impairment of health/developmental wellbeing
Types of abusePhysical, sexual, emotional, neglect, exploitation (CSE, criminal, online)
Children Acts1989/2004: duty to safeguard; Section 47 enquiry if risk of significant harm
Working TogetherMulti-agency guidance; named professionals; LADO for allegations against staff
Section 47 enquiryLocal authority investigation of risk of significant harm
Child protection planIf at continuing risk; CP conference, core group, plan review
Adolescent-specificExtra-familial harm, exploitation, county lines, online harms, self-neglect
CSE (child sexual exploitation)Under-18 in sexual activity in exchange for something; coercion key
County linesGangs recruiting children to carry drugs; exploitation and modern slavery
Information sharingFears of breaching data protection NOT a barrier to safeguarding

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence.

Adolescents are vulnerable to safeguarding concerns including physical, sexual and emotional abuse, neglect, exploitation (child sexual exploitation, county lines, online exploitation), radicalisation, and harm from outside the home. The Children Acts 1989/2004 require Local Authorities to safeguard children in need and protect those at risk of significant harm. The Working Together to Safeguard Children framework sets the multi-agency approach. Adolescent safeguarding is distinct from younger children - they are more likely to experience extra-familial harm, self-neglect, exploitation and mental health crises rather than neglect or physical abuse at home.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence?

Definition of harm: Significant harm = ill-treatment or impairment of health/developmental wellbeing. Types of abuse: Physical, sexual, emotional, neglect, exploitation (CSE, criminal, online). Children Acts: 1989/2004: duty to safeguard; Section 47 enquiry if risk of significant harm. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Working Together - Multi-agency guidance; named professionals; LADO for allegations against staff; Section 47 enquiry - Local authority investigation of risk of significant harm. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence?

Investigations include: Child protection plan - If at continuing risk; CP conference, core group, plan review; Adolescent-specific - Extra-familial harm, exploitation, county lines, online harms, self-neglect. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence?

First-line management: CSE (child sexual exploitation) - Under-18 in sexual activity in exchange for something; coercion key; County lines - Gangs recruiting children to carry drugs; exploitation and modern slavery. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence?

Red flags and complications: Information sharing - Fears of breaching data protection NOT a barrier to safeguarding. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Safeguarding and child protection in adolescence - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Delayed Puberty
topics.html:
Definition

Delayed puberty is defined as no secondary sexual characteristics by age 13 in girls (or 4 years post-thelarche) or age 14 in boys (or 5 years post-testicular enlargement). Causes are classified as hypogonadotropic (central, low LH/FSH) or hypergonadotropic (primary gonadal failure, high LH/FSH). Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) is the commonest cause in boys. Investigations include bone age, LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, prolactin, TSH, karyotype, MRI pituitary if central. Treatment depends on cause - observation for CDGP, sex-steroid replacement, or definitive management of underlying cause.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

No thelarche by 13 in girls; no testicular enlargement by 14 in boys

Most common cause

Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP), especially boys

Hypogonadotropic

Central; low LH/FSH; causes: CDGP, Kallmann, anorexia, hypopituitarism, chronic disease

Hypergonadotropic

Primary gonadal failure; high LH/FSH; causes: Turner, Klinefelter, chemo/radiotherapy

Kallmann syndrome

Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism + anosmia; KAL1, FGFR1 mutations

Turner syndrome

45,X; streak ovaries; primary amenorrhoea, short stature, webbed neck

Klinefelter syndrome

47,XXY; small firm testes, gynaecomastia, tall stature, infertility

Investigations

Bone age (X-ray wrist), LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, prolactin, TSH, karyotype

Treatment CDGP

Observation; short course oxandrolone (boys) or oestradiol (girls)

Permanent hypogonadism

Long-term sex-steroid replacement; fertility counselling

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionNo thelarche by 13 in girls; no testicular enlargement by 14 in boys

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Delayed puberty.

Delayed puberty is defined as no secondary sexual characteristics by age 13 in girls (or 4 years post-thelarche) or age 14 in boys (or 5 years post-testicular enlargement). Causes are classified as hypogonadotropic (central, low LH/FSH) or hypergonadotropic (primary gonadal failure, high LH/FSH). Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) is the commonest cause in boys. Investigations include bone age, LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, prolactin, TSH, karyotype, MRI pituitary if central. Treatment depends on cause - observation for CDGP, sex-steroid replacement, or definitive management of underlying cause.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Delayed puberty?

Definition: No thelarche by 13 in girls; no testicular enlargement by 14 in boys. Most common cause: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP), especially boys. Hypogonadotropic: Central; low LH/FSH; causes: CDGP, Kallmann, anorexia, hypopituitarism, chronic disease. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Delayed puberty recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Hypergonadotropic - Primary gonadal failure; high LH/FSH; causes: Turner, Klinefelter, chemo/radiotherapy; Kallmann syndrome - Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism + anosmia; KAL1, FGFR1 mutations. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Delayed puberty?

Investigations include: Turner syndrome - 45,X; streak ovaries; primary amenorrhoea, short stature, webbed neck; Klinefelter syndrome - 47,XXY; small firm testes, gynaecomastia, tall stature, infertility. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Delayed puberty?

First-line management: Investigations - Bone age (X-ray wrist), LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, prolactin, TSH, karyotype; Treatment CDGP - Observation; short course oxandrolone (boys) or oestradiol (girls). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Delayed puberty?

Red flags and complications: Permanent hypogonadism - Long-term sex-steroid replacement; fertility counselling. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Delayed puberty - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Normal Puberty And Pubertal Staging
topics.html:
Definition

Normal puberty is the orderly, timed sequence of sexual maturation driven by reactivation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, beginning with adrenarche (adrenal androgens, ~6-8 yrs) followed by gonadarche (HPG axis activation). Tanner/Marshall staging documents secondary sexual characteristics across 5 stages: breast (B1-B5) and pubic hair (PH1-PH5) in girls; genital (G1-G5), pubic hair (PH1-PH5) and testicular volume (Prader beads) in boys. The first signs are thelarche in girls (~10-11 yrs) and testicular enlargement in boys (~11-12 yrs). Menarche typically follows thelarche by 2-3 years.

Core Clinical Content

Tanner stages

5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult

Thelarche

First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2)

Menarche

Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4

Testicular volume

=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement

Pubic hair

PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche

Adrenarche

DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs

Gonadarche

HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids

Peak height velocity

Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4)

Bone age

X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constitutional delay

Sequence (girls)

Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Normal puberty and pubertal staging.

Normal puberty is the orderly, timed sequence of sexual maturation driven by reactivation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, beginning with adrenarche (adrenal androgens, ~6-8 yrs) followed by gonadarche (HPG axis activation). Tanner/Marshall staging documents secondary sexual characteristics across 5 stages: breast (B1-B5) and pubic hair (PH1-PH5) in girls; genital (G1-G5), pubic hair (PH1-PH5) and testicular volume (Prader beads) in boys. The first signs are thelarche in girls (~10-11 yrs) and testicular enlargement in boys (~11-12 yrs). Menarche typically follows thelarche by 2-3 years.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

Tanner stages: 5 stages; B1 prepubertal, B5 adult; G1 prepubertal, G5 adult. Thelarche: First sign in girls, ~10-11 yrs (range 8-13); breast bud (B2). Menarche: Average 12-13 yrs; follows thelarche by 2-3 years; Tanner B4. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Normal puberty and pubertal staging recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Testicular volume - >=4 mL (Prader) = first sign in boys; orchidometer measurement; Pubic hair - PH2 typically 11-12 in girls, 12-13 in boys; adrenarche. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

Investigations include: Adrenarche - DHEA, androstenedione from adrenal zona reticularis; 6-8 yrs; Gonadarche - HPG axis reactivation; pulsatile GnRH -> LH/FSH -> sex steroids. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

First-line management: Peak height velocity - Girls 11-12 yrs (Tanner B3); boys 13-14 yrs (Tanner G4); Bone age - X-ray left wrist; advanced in precocious, delayed in constitutional delay. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Normal puberty and pubertal staging?

Red flags and complications: Sequence (girls) - Thelarche -> pubarche -> growth spurt -> menarche. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Normal puberty and pubertal staging - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Precocious Puberty
topics.html:
Definition

Precocious puberty is the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in girls or 9 in boys. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent, true precocious puberty - premature HPG activation) or peripheral (GnRH-independent, false - sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous). Central is more common in girls (often idiopathic) and far more common in boys (CNS lesion until proven otherwise - hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma). Investigations: bone age (advanced), LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, 17-OH progesterone (CAH), GnRH stimulation test, MRI brain. Treatment depends on cause - GnRH analogues for central, treat underlying cause for peripheral.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Secondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys)

Central (true)

GnRH-dependent; premature HPG activation; idiopathic commonest in girls

Peripheral (false)

GnRH-independent; sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous

Boys with precocious

CNS lesion until proven otherwise - MRI brain mandatory

Hypothalamic hamartoma

Classic cause; gelastic seizures + central precocious puberty

CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia)

21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation in girls, premature in boys

McCune-Albright

Peripheral precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia

GnRH stimulation test

Central: LH rises >5 IU/L; Peripheral: no response

Treatment central

GnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC

Treatment peripheral

Treat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionSecondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys)
Central (true)GnRH-dependent; premature HPG activation; idiopathic commonest in girls
Peripheral (false)GnRH-independent; sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous

Treatment centralGnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC
Treatment peripheralTreat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Precocious puberty.

Precocious puberty is the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in girls or 9 in boys. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent, true precocious puberty - premature HPG activation) or peripheral (GnRH-independent, false - sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous). Central is more common in girls (often idiopathic) and far more common in boys (CNS lesion until proven otherwise - hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma). Investigations: bone age (advanced), LH/FSH, oestradiol/testosterone, 17-OH progesterone (CAH), GnRH stimulation test, MRI brain. Treatment depends on cause - GnRH analogues for central, treat underlying cause for peripheral.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Precocious puberty?

Definition: Secondary sexual characteristics <8 yrs (girls) or <9 yrs (boys). Central (true): GnRH-dependent; premature HPG activation; idiopathic commonest in girls. Peripheral (false): GnRH-independent; sex steroids from gonads/adrenals/exogenous. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Precocious puberty recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Boys with precocious - CNS lesion until proven otherwise - MRI brain mandatory; Hypothalamic hamartoma - Classic cause; gelastic seizures + central precocious puberty. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Precocious puberty?

Investigations include: CAH (congenital adrenal hyperplasia) - 21-hydroxylase deficiency; virilisation in girls, premature in boys; McCune-Albright - Peripheral precocious puberty + cafe-au-lait + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Precocious puberty?

First-line management: GnRH stimulation test - Central: LH rises >5 IU/L; Peripheral: no response; Treatment central - GnRH analogues (leuprorelin, triptorelin) - monthly/3-monthly IM/SC. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Precocious puberty?

Red flags and complications: Treatment peripheral - Treat underlying cause: CAH -> hydrocortisone; tumour -> surgery. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Precocious puberty - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Short Stature And Growth Disorders
topics.html:
Definition

Short stature is defined as height <2nd centile for age and sex, or >2 SD below the mean, or a height centile substantially below mid-parental height. Causes include familial short stature, constitutional delay of growth and puberty, endocrine (GH deficiency, hypothyroidism, Cushing), chronic disease (coeliac, IBD, CKD, cardiac), chromosomal (Turner), and skeletal dysplasias. Evaluation includes detailed history, growth-chart plotting, bone age, parental heights, bloods (FBC, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype in girls), and imaging. GH therapy is indicated for proven GH deficiency and specific syndromes (Turner, Prader-Willi, chronic renal failure, SGA without catch-up).

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Height <2nd centile or >2 SD below mean

Most common causes

Familial short stature + constitutional delay (normal variants)

Pathological causes

Endocrine (GH def, hypothyroid, Cushing), chronic disease, Turner, skeletal dysplasia

Mid-parental height

Boys: (dad+ mum +13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Girls: (mum + dad -13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm

Bone age

Delayed in CDGP, GH deficiency, hypothyroid; normal in familial

Investigations

FBC, ferritin, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype (girls), urinary organic acids

GH stimulation test

Clonidine, glucagon or insulin tolerance test; peak GH <7 ug/L = deficiency

GH therapy

Daily SC injection; indicated in GH def, Turner, Prader-Willi, SGA, CRF

Side effects GH

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, slipped epiphyses, hyperglycaemia

Tall stature

Causes: familial, Klinefelter, Marfan, Sotos; consider if >2 SD above mean

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionHeight <2nd centile or >2 SD below mean
Most common causesFamilial short stature + constitutional delay (normal variants)
Pathological causesEndocrine (GH def, hypothyroid, Cushing), chronic disease, Turner, skeletal dysplasia

Tall statureCauses: familial, Klinefelter, Marfan, Sotos; consider if >2 SD above mean

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Short stature and growth disorders.

Short stature is defined as height <2nd centile for age and sex, or >2 SD below the mean, or a height centile substantially below mid-parental height. Causes include familial short stature, constitutional delay of growth and puberty, endocrine (GH deficiency, hypothyroidism, Cushing), chronic disease (coeliac, IBD, CKD, cardiac), chromosomal (Turner), and skeletal dysplasias. Evaluation includes detailed history, growth-chart plotting, bone age, parental heights, bloods (FBC, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype in girls), and imaging. GH therapy is indicated for proven GH deficiency and specific syndromes (Turner, Prader-Willi, chronic renal failure, SGA without catch-up).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Short stature and growth disorders?

Definition: Height <2nd centile or >2 SD below mean. Most common causes: Familial short stature + constitutional delay (normal variants). Pathological causes: Endocrine (GH def, hypothyroid, Cushing), chronic disease, Turner, skeletal dysplasia. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Short stature and growth disorders recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Mid-parental height - Boys: (dad+ mum +13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Girls: (mum + dad -13)/2 +/- 8.5 cm; Bone age - Delayed in CDGP, GH deficiency, hypothyroid; normal in familial. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Short stature and growth disorders?

Investigations include: Investigations - FBC, ferritin, TFTs, IGF-1, coeliac serology, karyotype (girls), urinary organic acids; GH stimulation test - Clonidine, glucagon or insulin tolerance test; peak GH <7 ug/L = deficiency. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Short stature and growth disorders?

First-line management: GH therapy - Daily SC injection; indicated in GH def, Turner, Prader-Willi, SGA, CRF; Side effects GH - Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, slipped epiphyses, hyperglycaemia. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Short stature and growth disorders?

Red flags and complications: Tall stature - Causes: familial, Klinefelter, Marfan, Sotos; consider if >2 SD above mean. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Short stature and growth disorders - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Contraception In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Contraception in adolescents requires confidential, individualised counselling covering efficacy, side effects, reversibility, and STI prevention. Long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) - IUS, IUD, implant - is first-line for most adolescents due to highest efficacy and adherence. Combined oral contraception (COC) is widely used; progestogen-only pill (POP) is suitable when oestrogen contraindicated. Emergency contraception (copper IUD most effective; ulipristal/levonorgestrel) within 5 days. Condoms are essential for STI prevention. UK: under-13 sexual activity mandates safeguarding referral; 13-15 assessed case-by-case.

Core Clinical Content

LARC first-line

IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99%

Implanon/Nexplanon

Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common

IUS Mirena

Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea

COC

21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, mood effects

POP

Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-hr window

DMPA (depot)

3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh benefit

Emergency contraception

Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), levonorgestrel (3 days)

Condoms

Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal

Fraser guidelines

Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature minor

Sexual activity <13

Mandatory safeguarding referral

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Contraception in adolescents.

Contraception in adolescents requires confidential, individualised counselling covering efficacy, side effects, reversibility, and STI prevention. Long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) - IUS, IUD, implant - is first-line for most adolescents due to highest efficacy and adherence. Combined oral contraception (COC) is widely used; progestogen-only pill (POP) is suitable when oestrogen contraindicated. Emergency contraception (copper IUD most effective; ulipristal/levonorgestrel) within 5 days. Condoms are essential for STI prevention. UK: under-13 sexual activity mandates safeguarding referral; 13-15 assessed case-by-case.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Contraception in adolescents?

LARC first-line: IUS (Mirena), IUD, implant (Nexplanon) - efficacy >99%. Implanon/Nexplanon: Subdermal etonogestrel; 3 years; amenorrhoea common. IUS Mirena: Levonorgestrel-releasing; 5 years; reduced bleeding, dysmenorrhoea. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Contraception in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: COC - 21 days + 7-day break; missed pill rules; VTE risk, weight, mood effects; POP - Cerazette (desogestrel) - 12-hr window; traditional POPs 3-hr window. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Contraception in adolescents?

Investigations include: DMPA (depot) - 3-monthly injection; reduced BMD with prolonged use; weigh benefit; Emergency contraception - Copper IUD (most effective, 5 days), ulipristal (5 days), levonorgestrel (3 days). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Contraception in adolescents?

First-line management: Condoms - Essential for STI prevention; dual method with hormonal ideal; Fraser guidelines - Under-16 contraception without parental knowledge if mature minor. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Contraception in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Sexual activity <13 - Mandatory safeguarding referral. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Contraception in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Sexual Health And Gender Identity
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescent sexual and gender identity development is a normal part of adolescence, with sexual orientation (who one is attracted to), gender identity (ones sense of self as male, female, both, neither) and gender expression (how one presents) evolving through adolescence. LGBT+ adolescents have higher rates of mental health problems, self-harm, substance use, and homelessness due to stigma and minority stress. Gender dysphoria requires specialist multidisciplinary input (CAMHS, endocrinology for puberty suppression, surgical). All adolescents need a safe, confidential space to discuss identity.

Core Clinical Content

Sexual orientation

Lesbian, gay, bisexual, queer (LGBQ); usually emerges mid-adolescence

Gender identity

Internal sense; distinct from assigned sex at birth

Transgender

Gender identity differs from assigned sex; may experience dysphoria

Non-binary

Gender identity outside male/female binary

Coming out

Process of disclosing identity; varies; may be safe or risky

Minority stress

Stigma, discrimination, rejection drive higher mental health risk

Mental health

LGBTQ+ 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide

Puberty suppression

GnRH analogues (triptorelin) for adolescents with persistent gender dysphoria

CAMHS

Child and adolescent mental health service; specialist gender clinics (GIDS in UK)

Supportive care

Affirming name/pronouns, family acceptance, school support - improves outcomes

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Sexual orientationLesbian, gay, bisexual, queer (LGBQ); usually emerges mid-adolescence
Gender identityInternal sense; distinct from assigned sex at birth
TransgenderGender identity differs from assigned sex; may experience dysphoria
Non-binaryGender identity outside male/female binary
Coming outProcess of disclosing identity; varies; may be safe or risky
Minority stressStigma, discrimination, rejection drive higher mental health risk
Mental healthLGBTQ+ 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide
Puberty suppressionGnRH analogues (triptorelin) for adolescents with persistent gender dysphoria
CAMHSChild and adolescent mental health service; specialist gender clinics (GIDS in UK)
Supportive careAffirming name/pronouns, family acceptance, school support - improves outcomes

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Sexual health and gender identity.

Adolescent sexual and gender identity development is a normal part of adolescence, with sexual orientation (who one is attracted to), gender identity (ones sense of self as male, female, both, neither) and gender expression (how one presents) evolving through adolescence. LGBT+ adolescents have higher rates of mental health problems, self-harm, substance use, and homelessness due to stigma and minority stress. Gender dysphoria requires specialist multidisciplinary input (CAMHS, endocrinology for puberty suppression, surgical). All adolescents need a safe, confidential space to discuss identity.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Sexual health and gender identity?

Sexual orientation: Lesbian, gay, bisexual, queer (LGBQ); usually emerges mid-adolescence. Gender identity: Internal sense; distinct from assigned sex at birth. Transgender: Gender identity differs from assigned sex; may experience dysphoria. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Sexual health and gender identity recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Non-binary - Gender identity outside male/female binary; Coming out - Process of disclosing identity; varies; may be safe or risky. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Sexual health and gender identity?

Investigations include: Minority stress - Stigma, discrimination, rejection drive higher mental health risk; Mental health - LGBTQ+ 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Sexual health and gender identity?

First-line management: Puberty suppression - GnRH analogues (triptorelin) for adolescents with persistent gender dysphoria; CAMHS - Child and adolescent mental health service; specialist gender clinics (GIDS in UK). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Sexual health and gender identity?

Red flags and complications: Supportive care - Affirming name/pronouns, family acceptance, school support - improves outcomes. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sexual health and gender identity - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Sexually Transmitted Infections In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescents (15-24 yrs) have the highest rates of STIs in the UK and globally, accounting for ~50% of new infections. Biological vulnerability (cervical ectopy, immune naivety), behavioural factors (multiple partners, inconsistent condom use, substance use), and barriers to testing all contribute. Common STIs: chlamydia, gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis (rising), HIV. Screening: chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal swab), HIV, syphilis serology. Treatment: single-dose antibiotics for chlamydia (azithromycin 1g) or 7-day doxycycline; partner notification; vaccination (HPV, Hep B, HAV).

Core Clinical Content

STI rates

50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds

Common STIs

Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas

Asymptomatic chlamydia

Up to 70% in females; 50% in males

Complications

PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis

Screening

Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active

Chlamydia treatment

Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)

Gonorrhoea treatment

Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)

Partner notification

Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas

HPV vaccine

School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer

HIV PrEP

Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
STI rates50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds
Common STIsChlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas
Asymptomatic chlamydiaUp to 70% in females; 50% in males
ComplicationsPID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis
ScreeningAnnual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active
Chlamydia treatmentDoxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat)
Gonorrhoea treatmentCeftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual)
Partner notificationTrace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas
HPV vaccineSchool-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer
HIV PrEPPre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents.

Adolescents (15-24 yrs) have the highest rates of STIs in the UK and globally, accounting for ~50% of new infections. Biological vulnerability (cervical ectopy, immune naivety), behavioural factors (multiple partners, inconsistent condom use, substance use), and barriers to testing all contribute. Common STIs: chlamydia, gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis (rising), HIV. Screening: chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal swab), HIV, syphilis serology. Treatment: single-dose antibiotics for chlamydia (azithromycin 1g) or 7-day doxycycline; partner notification; vaccination (HPV, Hep B, HAV).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

STI rates: 50% of new STIs occur in 15-24 yr-olds. Common STIs: Chlamydia (most common), gonorrhoea, HPV, HSV, syphilis, HIV, trichomonas. Asymptomatic chlamydia: Up to 70% in females; 50% in males. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Complications - PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis; Screening - Annual chlamydia NAAT (urine/vulvovaginal) in <25 sexually active. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

Investigations include: Chlamydia treatment - Doxycycline 100 mg BD 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g stat); Gonorrhoea treatment - Ceftriaxone IM stat + azithromycin 1 g (dual). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

First-line management: Partner notification - Trace contacts in last 3-6 months; expedited partner therapy in some areas; HPV vaccine - School-based programme; 9-valent; 12-13 yr-olds; effective against cervical cancer. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: HIV PrEP - Pre-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk MSM/trans; tenofovir/emtricitabine. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Sexually transmitted infections in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Adolescent Sexual And Gender Minority Health
topics.html:
Definition

LGBTQ+ (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer/questioning, +) adolescents experience unique health disparities driven by minority stress, family rejection, and discrimination. They have 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts; higher rates of substance use, homelessness, and victimisation. Protective factors: family acceptance, school support, affirming healthcare. Gender dysphoria requires specialist input; puberty suppression, cross-sex hormones, and surgery are staged interventions. Mental health support is core to care, not optional.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+

Mental health

2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts

Substance use

Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping

Homelessness

20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)

Family acceptance

Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x

Affirming care

Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training

Gender dysphoria

Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input

Puberty suppression

GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible

Cross-sex hormones

From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys

Surgery

Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+
Mental health2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts
Substance useHigher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping
Homelessness20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection)
Family acceptanceStrongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x
Affirming careUse chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training
Gender dysphoriaDistress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input
Puberty suppressionGnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible
Cross-sex hormonesFrom ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys
SurgeryGenital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Adolescent sexual and gender minority health.

LGBTQ+ (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer/questioning, +) adolescents experience unique health disparities driven by minority stress, family rejection, and discrimination. They have 2-3x higher rates of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts; higher rates of substance use, homelessness, and victimisation. Protective factors: family acceptance, school support, affirming healthcare. Gender dysphoria requires specialist input; puberty suppression, cross-sex hormones, and surgery are staged interventions. Mental health support is core to care, not optional.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

Prevalence: 5-10% of adolescents identify as LGBT+. Mental health: 2-3x risk of depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicide attempts. Substance use: Higher rates of smoking, alcohol, drug use as coping. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adolescent sexual and gender minority health recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Homelessness - 20-40% of homeless youth identify as LGBT+ (family rejection); Family acceptance - Strongest protective factor; reduces suicide risk 8x. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

Investigations include: Affirming care - Use chosen name/pronouns; safe clinical environment; staff training; Gender dysphoria - Distress from incongruence; specialist CAMHS/GIDS input. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

First-line management: Puberty suppression - GnRH analogues (triptorelin) at Tanner 2; reversible; Cross-sex hormones - From ~16 yrs; oestradiol for trans girls, testosterone for trans boys. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adolescent sexual and gender minority health?

Red flags and complications: Surgery - Genital surgery usually >=18; chest surgery can be earlier. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adolescent sexual and gender minority health - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)
