topics.title: Adolescents With Disability And Chronic Illness
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescents with physical, learning, or developmental disabilities face higher rates of health problems, mental health issues, social isolation, and barriers to healthcare. They may have age-specific concerns about puberty (period management, fertility, sexual function), transition, education (EHCP in UK), employment, independent living, and social relationships. Care is multidisciplinary (paediatrician, GP, OT, physio, SALT, social work, special educational needs, mental health). Reasonable adjustments (Equality Act 2010) are mandatory. Transition to adult services is often more complex and starts earlier.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

7-10% of adolescents have a disability

Categories

Physical (CP, spina bifida), sensory (visual, hearing), learning (ID, ASD), neuro (epilepsy)

Puberty

Delayed or precocious in some; period management, fertility, sexual function

Mental health

Higher rates of depression, anxiety, social isolation; screening essential

EHCP (UK)

Education, Health and Care Plan; 0-25 yrs; legal framework

Reasonable adjustments

Equality Act 2010; accessible premises, communication, longer appointments

Mental Capacity Act

16+; assume capacity; supported decision-making

Transition

More complex; adult disability services, social care, employment support

Carers

Parents/carers need support; young carer assessment; respite

Sexual health

Often overlooked; Fraser principles still apply; contraception, STI prevention

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence7-10% of adolescents have a disability
CategoriesPhysical (CP, spina bifida), sensory (visual, hearing), learning (ID, ASD), neuro (epilepsy)
PubertyDelayed or precocious in some; period management, fertility, sexual function
Mental healthHigher rates of depression, anxiety, social isolation; screening essential
EHCP (UK)Education, Health and Care Plan; 0-25 yrs; legal framework
Reasonable adjustmentsEquality Act 2010; accessible premises, communication, longer appointments
Mental Capacity Act16+; assume capacity; supported decision-making
TransitionMore complex; adult disability services, social care, employment support
CarersParents/carers need support; young carer assessment; respite
Sexual healthOften overlooked; Fraser principles still apply; contraception, STI prevention

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Adolescents with disability and chronic illness.

Adolescents with physical, learning, or developmental disabilities face higher rates of health problems, mental health issues, social isolation, and barriers to healthcare. They may have age-specific concerns about puberty (period management, fertility, sexual function), transition, education (EHCP in UK), employment, independent living, and social relationships. Care is multidisciplinary (paediatrician, GP, OT, physio, SALT, social work, special educational needs, mental health). Reasonable adjustments (Equality Act 2010) are mandatory. Transition to adult services is often more complex and starts earlier.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

Prevalence: 7-10% of adolescents have a disability. Categories: Physical (CP, spina bifida), sensory (visual, hearing), learning (ID, ASD), neuro (epilepsy). Puberty: Delayed or precocious in some; period management, fertility, sexual function. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adolescents with disability and chronic illness recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Mental health - Higher rates of depression, anxiety, social isolation; screening essential; EHCP (UK) - Education, Health and Care Plan; 0-25 yrs; legal framework. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

Investigations include: Reasonable adjustments - Equality Act 2010; accessible premises, communication, longer appointments; Mental Capacity Act - 16+; assume capacity; supported decision-making. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

First-line management: Transition - More complex; adult disability services, social care, employment support; Carers - Parents/carers need support; young carer assessment; respite. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adolescents with disability and chronic illness?

Red flags and complications: Sexual health - Often overlooked; Fraser principles still apply; contraception, STI prevention. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adolescents with disability and chronic illness - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Models Of Transition Care
topics.html:
Definition

Several transition models exist, ranging from simple transfer to joint clinics to dedicated transition services. Evidence favours structured, multi-component interventions with both paediatric and adult team involvement. Joint clinics (paediatric and adult teams co-consult) reduce disengagement. Transition coordinators / navigators improve attendance and self-management. Disease-specific transition programmes (e.g., Cystic Fibrosis, Congenital Heart Disease, Diabetes, JIA) are most developed. Universal transition principles apply to all long-term conditions; service design must be flexible for less common conditions.

Core Clinical Content

Joint clinics

Paediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces

Transition coordinator

Named professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up

Disease-specific programmes

CF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed

Young adult clinics

Adult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs)

Ready Steady Go

10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services

Hello/Hands-off

Adult team gradually takes over from paediatric

Telephone/virtual follow-up

Improves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults

Peer support

Peer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities

Mental health integration

Embedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition

Evaluation

PDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control, satisfaction

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Joint clinicsPaediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces
Transition coordinatorNamed professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up
Disease-specific programmesCF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed
Young adult clinicsAdult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs)
Ready Steady Go10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services
Hello/Hands-offAdult team gradually takes over from paediatric
Telephone/virtual follow-upImproves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults
Peer supportPeer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities
Mental health integrationEmbedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition
EvaluationPDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control, satisfaction

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Models of transition care.

Several transition models exist, ranging from simple transfer to joint clinics to dedicated transition services. Evidence favours structured, multi-component interventions with both paediatric and adult team involvement. Joint clinics (paediatric and adult teams co-consult) reduce disengagement. Transition coordinators / navigators improve attendance and self-management. Disease-specific transition programmes (e.g., Cystic Fibrosis, Congenital Heart Disease, Diabetes, JIA) are most developed. Universal transition principles apply to all long-term conditions; service design must be flexible for less common conditions.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Models of transition care?

Joint clinics: Paediatric + adult team co-consult; reduces disengagement; familiar faces. Transition coordinator: Named professional; supports navigation, planning, follow-up. Disease-specific programmes: CF, CHD, diabetes, JIA, IBD - most developed. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Models of transition care recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Young adult clinics - Adult setting, but with developmental flexibility (e.g., 16-25 yrs); Ready Steady Go - 10-step transition programme widely used in UK paediatric services. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Models of transition care?

Investigations include: Hello/Hands-off - Adult team gradually takes over from paediatric; Telephone/virtual follow-up - Improves engagement with hard-to-reach young adults. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Models of transition care?

First-line management: Peer support - Peer mentors, social media support groups, condition-specific charities; Mental health integration - Embedded CAMHS or adult mental health during transition. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Models of transition care?

Red flags and complications: Evaluation - PDSA cycles; outcome measures: attendance, disease control, satisfaction. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Models of transition care - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Transition Of Care Principles
topics.html:
Definition

Transition is the purposeful, planned movement of adolescents and young adults with chronic conditions from child-centred to adult-centred healthcare. Distinct from transfer (the actual event of moving), transition is a multi-year process. Failures in transition are associated with disengagement, non-adherence, emergency admissions and increased morbidity. National guidance (NICE 2016, Got Transition, AAP/AAFP/ACP consensus 2011) outlines 6 core elements: transition policy, tracking, readiness assessment, planning, transfer of care, and completion. Transition should be developmentally appropriate, coordinated, and start in early adolescence (12-14 yrs).

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Planned, purposeful process from child- to adult-centred care

Transition vs transfer

Transition = process; Transfer = event

6 core elements

Policy, Tracking, Readiness assessment, Planning, Transfer, Completion

NICE NG43 (2016)

UK guidance; transition planning from age 13-14

Got Transition

US six-core-elements framework; widely used

Timing

Start planning ~12-14 yrs; transfer typically 16-19 yrs; flexible

Outcome data

Poor transition -> non-adherence, disengagement, ED visits, morbidity

Multidisciplinary

Paediatric + adult teams + GP + adolescent + family

Named worker

Transition coordinator helps navigation; improves outcomes

Quality indicators

Successful transfer, attendance at adult clinic, disease control, satisfaction

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionPlanned, purposeful process from child- to adult-centred care
Transition vs transferTransition = process; Transfer = event
6 core elementsPolicy, Tracking, Readiness assessment, Planning, Transfer, Completion
NICE NG43 (2016)UK guidance; transition planning from age 13-14
Got TransitionUS six-core-elements framework; widely used
TimingStart planning ~12-14 yrs; transfer typically 16-19 yrs; flexible
Outcome dataPoor transition -> non-adherence, disengagement, ED visits, morbidity
MultidisciplinaryPaediatric + adult teams + GP + adolescent + family
Named workerTransition coordinator helps navigation; improves outcomes
Quality indicatorsSuccessful transfer, attendance at adult clinic, disease control, satisfaction

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Transition of care principles.

Transition is the purposeful, planned movement of adolescents and young adults with chronic conditions from child-centred to adult-centred healthcare. Distinct from transfer (the actual event of moving), transition is a multi-year process. Failures in transition are associated with disengagement, non-adherence, emergency admissions and increased morbidity. National guidance (NICE 2016, Got Transition, AAP/AAFP/ACP consensus 2011) outlines 6 core elements: transition policy, tracking, readiness assessment, planning, transfer of care, and completion. Transition should be developmentally appropriate, coordinated, and start in early adolescence (12-14 yrs).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Transition of care principles?

Definition: Planned, purposeful process from child- to adult-centred care. Transition vs transfer: Transition = process; Transfer = event. 6 core elements: Policy, Tracking, Readiness assessment, Planning, Transfer, Completion. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Transition of care principles recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: NICE NG43 (2016) - UK guidance; transition planning from age 13-14; Got Transition - US six-core-elements framework; widely used. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Transition of care principles?

Investigations include: Timing - Start planning ~12-14 yrs; transfer typically 16-19 yrs; flexible; Outcome data - Poor transition -> non-adherence, disengagement, ED visits, morbidity. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Transition of care principles?

First-line management: Multidisciplinary - Paediatric + adult teams + GP + adolescent + family; Named worker - Transition coordinator helps navigation; improves outcomes. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Transition of care principles?

topics.title: Transition Readiness Assessment
topics.html:
Definition

Transition readiness assessment evaluates the adolescents knowledge, skills, and self-management behaviours needed for adult healthcare. Validated tools include the STARx (Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood Questionnaire), Am I ON TRAC, and Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire (TRAQ). Domains: disease knowledge, medication management, communication with providers, navigation of healthcare, self-advocacy, daily living skills. Assessment should start in early adolescence and repeat over time, informing transition planning. Health literacy, executive function, neurodevelopmental conditions (autism, ADHD) and learning disability need to be considered.

Core Clinical Content

Tools

STARx, TRAQ, Am I ON TRAC, Ready Steady Go

Domains

Disease knowledge, medication, communication, navigation, self-advocacy, daily living

Timing

Start at 12-14; repeat at 14-16, 16-18

STARx

Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood with Rx = medication domain

Health literacy

Understanding of own condition, treatments, when to seek help

Executive function

Planning, organisation, problem-solving; assess in ADHD, learning disability

Self-advocacy

Ability to ask questions, raise concerns, express preferences

Communication

Talking to clinicians alone, telephone triage, asking about results

Practical

Own medications, prescription refills, appointment booking, transport

Reasonable adjustments

Mental capacity assessment, easy-read materials, advocates

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ToolsSTARx, TRAQ, Am I ON TRAC, Ready Steady Go
DomainsDisease knowledge, medication, communication, navigation, self-advocacy, daily living
TimingStart at 12-14; repeat at 14-16, 16-18

Reasonable adjustmentsMental capacity assessment, easy-read materials, advocates

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Transition readiness assessment.

Transition readiness assessment evaluates the adolescents knowledge, skills, and self-management behaviours needed for adult healthcare. Validated tools include the STARx (Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood Questionnaire), Am I ON TRAC, and Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire (TRAQ). Domains: disease knowledge, medication management, communication with providers, navigation of healthcare, self-advocacy, daily living skills. Assessment should start in early adolescence and repeat over time, informing transition planning. Health literacy, executive function, neurodevelopmental conditions (autism, ADHD) and learning disability need to be considered.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Transition readiness assessment?

Tools: STARx, TRAQ, Am I ON TRAC, Ready Steady Go. Domains: Disease knowledge, medication, communication, navigation, self-advocacy, daily living. Timing: Start at 12-14; repeat at 14-16, 16-18. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Transition readiness assessment recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: STARx - Self-Management and Transition to Adulthood with Rx = medication domain; Health literacy - Understanding of own condition, treatments, when to seek help. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Transition readiness assessment?

Investigations include: Executive function - Planning, organisation, problem-solving; assess in ADHD, learning disability; Self-advocacy - Ability to ask questions, raise concerns, express preferences. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Transition readiness assessment?

First-line management: Communication - Talking to clinicians alone, telephone triage, asking about results; Practical - Own medications, prescription refills, appointment booking, transport. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Transition readiness assessment?

Red flags and complications: Reasonable adjustments - Mental capacity assessment, easy-read materials, advocates. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Transition readiness assessment - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Adolescent Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescent pregnancy (10-19 years) is associated with higher maternal and neonatal complications - pre-eclampsia, preterm birth, low birth weight, caesarean section, postnatal depression, and social/educational disruption. UK has the highest teenage pregnancy rate in Western Europe. Risk factors include deprivation, low educational attainment, sexual abuse, substance use, and mental health issues. Management requires a multidisciplinary team (obstetrician, midwife, paediatrician, social work, mental health). Contraception, antenatal care, safeguarding, and post-natal support are the four pillars. Confidential care and Fraser guidelines apply.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence (UK)

Declining; ~13/1000 in under-18s; highest in Western Europe historically

Risk factors

Deprivation, low education, sexual abuse, mental health, substance use, looked-after children

Complications maternal

Pre-eclampsia, anaemia, postnatal depression, social disruption

Complications fetal

Preterm, low birth weight, SGA, higher perinatal mortality

Antenatal care

Same as adults but multidisciplinary, mental health screening, safeguarding

Safeguarding

Under-18 mothers often have own safeguarding history; assessment mandatory

Mental health

Perinatal mental health screening at booking and 6 weeks

Contraception post-natal

Discussed antenatally; LARC (depot/IUS) suitable for adolescent mothers

Termination

Available; ground C (1974) most common reason; FPA framework

Prevention

Comprehensive RSE, LARC access, school-based clinics, support for looked-after children

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Risk factorsDeprivation, low education, sexual abuse, mental health, substance use, looked-after children

Complications fetalPreterm, low birth weight, SGA, higher perinatal mortality
Antenatal careSame as adults but multidisciplinary, mental health screening, safeguarding
SafeguardingUnder-18 mothers often have own safeguarding history; assessment mandatory

UK has the highest teenage pregnancy rate in Western Europe. Risk factors include deprivation, low educational attainment, sexual abuse, substance use, and mental health issues. Management requires a multidisciplinary team (obstetrician, midwife, paediatrician, social work, mental health). Contraception, antenatal care, safeguarding, and post-natal support are the four pillars. Confidential care and Fraser guidelines apply.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Adolescent pregnancy?

Prevalence (UK): Declining; ~13/1000 in under-18s; highest in Western Europe historically. Risk factors: Deprivation, low education, sexual abuse, mental health, substance use, looked-after children. Complications maternal: Pre-eclampsia, anaemia, postnatal depression, social disruption. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Adolescent pregnancy recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Complications fetal - Preterm, low birth weight, SGA, higher perinatal mortality; Antenatal care - Same as adults but multidisciplinary, mental health screening, safeguarding. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Adolescent pregnancy?

Investigations include: Safeguarding - Under-18 mothers often have own safeguarding history; assessment mandatory; Mental health - Perinatal mental health screening at booking and 6 weeks. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Adolescent pregnancy?

First-line management: Contraception post-natal - Discussed antenatally; LARC (depot/IUS) suitable for adolescent mothers; Termination - Available; ground C (1974) most common reason; FPA framework. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Adolescent pregnancy?

Red flags and complications: Prevention - Comprehensive RSE, LARC access, school-based clinics, support for looked-after children. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Adolescent pregnancy - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Menstrual Disorders In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Menstrual disorders are common in adolescents due to immaturity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis in the first 2-3 years post-menarche. The most common presentations are primary amenorrhoea, secondary amenorrhoea, oligomenorrhoea, heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB), and dysmenorrhoea. After 2-3 years of regular cycles, anovulatory cycles become less common and pathology (PCOS, pregnancy, bleeding disorders, thyroid disease) should be considered. The PALM-COEIN classification is used for HMB. Pelvic ultrasound and examination are key investigations; management depends on cause and patient age.

Core Clinical Content

Anovulation common

HPO axis immature first 2-3 years post-menarche

Primary amenorrhoea

No menses by 15 (or 3 years post-thelarche); 13 with no secondary sexual characteristics

Causes primary

Constitutional delay, hypogonadotropic, hypergonadotropic, Mullerian agenesis, imperforate hymen

Secondary amenorrhoea

No menses >=3-6 months after cycles established

Causes secondary

Pregnancy, PCOS, hypothalamic (stress/weight/exercise), thyroid, hyperprolactinaemia

HMB (heavy menstrual bleeding)

PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy / Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not classified

Bleeding disorders

Von Willebrand disease in 10-20% of adolescent HMB

Dysmenorrhoea

Primary: prostaglandin-mediated, NSAIDs first-line; Secondary: endometriosis, fibroids

Endometriosis

Possible in adolescents with severe primary dysmenorrhoea; responds to NSAIDs/OCP

Management

Treat underlying; tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, hormonal (OCP, Mirena)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Anovulation commonHPO axis immature first 2-3 years post-menarche
Primary amenorrhoeaNo menses by 15 (or 3 years post-thelarche); 13 with no secondary sexual characteristics

Secondary amenorrhoeaNo menses >=3-6 months after cycles established
Causes secondaryPregnancy, PCOS, hypothalamic (stress/weight/exercise), thyroid, hyperprolactinaemia
HMB (heavy menstrual bleeding)PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy / Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not classified

DysmenorrhoeaPrimary: prostaglandin-mediated, NSAIDs first-line; Secondary: endometriosis, fibroids
EndometriosisPossible in adolescents with severe primary dysmenorrhoea; responds to NSAIDs/OCP
ManagementTreat underlying; tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, hormonal (OCP, Mirena)

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Menstrual disorders in adolescents.

Menstrual disorders are common in adolescents due to immaturity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis in the first 2-3 years post-menarche. The most common presentations are primary amenorrhoea, secondary amenorrhoea, oligomenorrhoea, heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB), and dysmenorrhoea. After 2-3 years of regular cycles, anovulatory cycles become less common and pathology (PCOS, pregnancy, bleeding disorders, thyroid disease) should be considered. The PALM-COEIN classification is used for HMB. Pelvic ultrasound and examination are key investigations; management depends on cause and patient age.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Menstrual disorders in adolescents?

Anovulation common: HPO axis immature first 2-3 years post-menarche. Primary amenorrhoea: No menses by 15 (or 3 years post-thelarche); 13 with no secondary sexual characteristics. Causes primary: Constitutional delay, hypogonadotropic, hypergonadotropic, Mullerian agenesis, imperforate hymen. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Menstrual disorders in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Secondary amenorrhoea - No menses >=3-6 months after cycles established; Causes secondary - Pregnancy, PCOS, hypothalamic (stress/weight/exercise), thyroid, hyperprolactinaemia. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Menstrual disorders in adolescents?

Investigations include: HMB (heavy menstrual bleeding) - PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy / Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not classified; Bleeding disorders - Von Willebrand disease in 10-20% of adolescent HMB. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Menstrual disorders in adolescents?

First-line management: Dysmenorrhoea - Primary: prostaglandin-mediated, NSAIDs first-line; Secondary: endometriosis, fibroids; Endometriosis - Possible in adolescents with severe primary dysmenorrhoea; responds to NSAIDs/OCP. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Menstrual disorders in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Management - Treat underlying; tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, hormonal (OCP, Mirena). Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Menstrual disorders in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

A: Constitutional delay, hypogonadotropic, hypergonadotropic, Mullerian agenesis, imperforate hymen

  • Q: Secondary amenorrhoea?

A: No menses >=3-6 months after cycles established

  • Q: Causes secondary?

A: Pregnancy, PCOS, hypothalamic (stress/weight/exercise), thyroid, hyperprolactinaemia

  • Q: HMB (heavy menstrual bleeding)?

A: PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy / Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not classified

  • Q: Bleeding disorders?

A: Von Willebrand disease in 10-20% of adolescent HMB

  • Q: Dysmenorrhoea?

A: Primary: prostaglandin-mediated, NSAIDs first-line; Secondary: endometriosis, fibroids

topics.title: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is the commonest endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age, with onset commonly in adolescence. It is defined by the Rotterdam criteria (>=2 of: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism [clinical or biochemical], polycystic ovaries on ultrasound), with adult criteria requiring exclusion of other causes. In adolescents, ultrasound is less reliable (PCOM appearance is normal in 30% of adolescents), so diagnosis is clinical - persistent menstrual irregularity >2 years post-menarche plus hyperandrogenism. PCOS is associated with insulin resistance, obesity, type 2 diabetes, infertility, and increased cardiovascular risk. Management is lifestyle first, hormonal regulation, and metformin in select cases.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

8-13% of reproductive-age women

Rotterdam criteria

=2 of: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries

Adolescent diagnosis

Clinical: persistent menstrual irregularity >2 yrs post-menarche + hyperandrogenism

Hyperandrogenism clinical

Hirsutism (modified Ferriman-Gallwey >=8), acne, alopecia

Hyperandrogenism biochemical

Increased free testosterone, increased androstenedione; LH:FSH ratio (2:1)

PCOS phenotypes

A (all 3), B (anovulation+HA), C (HA+PCOM), D (anovulation+PCOM)

Insulin resistance

Common; check fasting glucose/insulin, OGTT in obese

Differential

CAH (17-OH progesterone), androgen-secreting tumour, Cushing, thyroid

First-line management

Lifestyle: weight loss 5-10% restores ovulation

Pharmacological

OCP (cyclical regulation), metformin (IR/T2DM), spironolactone (hirsutism)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents.

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is the commonest endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age, with onset commonly in adolescence. It is defined by the Rotterdam criteria (>=2 of: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism [clinical or biochemical], polycystic ovaries on ultrasound), with adult criteria requiring exclusion of other causes. In adolescents, ultrasound is less reliable (PCOM appearance is normal in 30% of adolescents), so diagnosis is clinical - persistent menstrual irregularity >2 years post-menarche plus hyperandrogenism. PCOS is associated with insulin resistance, obesity, type 2 diabetes, infertility, and increased cardiovascular risk. Management is lifestyle first, hormonal regulation, and metformin in select cases.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents?

Prevalence: 8-13% of reproductive-age women. Rotterdam criteria: >=2 of: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries. Adolescent diagnosis: Clinical: persistent menstrual irregularity >2 yrs post-menarche + hyperandrogenism. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Hyperandrogenism clinical - Hirsutism (modified Ferriman-Gallwey >=8), acne, alopecia; Hyperandrogenism biochemical - Increased free testosterone, increased androstenedione; LH:FSH ratio (2:1). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents?

Investigations include: PCOS phenotypes - A (all 3), B (anovulation+HA), C (HA+PCOM), D (anovulation+PCOM); Insulin resistance - Common; check fasting glucose/insulin, OGTT in obese. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents?

First-line management: Differential - CAH (17-OH progesterone), androgen-secreting tumour, Cushing, thyroid; First-line management - Lifestyle: weight loss 5-10% restores ovulation. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Pharmacological - OCP (cyclical regulation), metformin (IR/T2DM), spironolactone (hirsutism). Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Polycystic ovary syndrome in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Acne In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Acne vulgaris is the commonest skin disease in adolescents, affecting 80-90% of teenagers, with significant psychological impact (depression, anxiety, low self-esteem, social withdrawal). Pathogenesis: follicular hyperkeratinisation, sebum production, Cutibacterium acnes, inflammation. Comedonal, inflammatory, and nodulocystic forms. Treatment is severity-based: mild (topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide), moderate (add topical/oral antibiotics - doxycycline, lymecycline), severe (isotretinoin, only under specialist supervision with pregnancy prevention programme). Hormonal therapy (OCP, spironolactone) in females.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

80-90% of adolescents; peak 14-17 yrs (girls) / 16-19 yrs (boys)

Pathogenesis

Follicular hyperkeratinisation, increased sebum, C. acnes, inflammation

Severity grading

Mild (comedonal), moderate (inflammatory), severe (nodulocystic)

Psychological impact

Depression, anxiety, low self-esteem, social withdrawal, suicidality

Mild treatment

Topical retinoid (adapalene) +/- benzoyl peroxide (BPO)

Moderate treatment

Add topical/oral antibiotic (doxycycline 100 mg, lymecycline 408 mg, 3 months)

Severe treatment

Oral isotretinoin (0.5-1 mg/kg) - 16-24 weeks; cumulative 120-150 mg/kg

Isotretinoin monitoring

LFTs, lipids, mood; pregnancy prevention (PPP - iPLEDGE in US)

Hormonal therapy

Combined OCP (with cyproterone/levonorgestrel), spironolactone (off-label)

Scarring

Treat early; isotretinoin for scarring acne regardless of severity

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence80-90% of adolescents; peak 14-17 yrs (girls) / 16-19 yrs (boys)
PathogenesisFollicular hyperkeratinisation, increased sebum, C. acnes, inflammation

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Acne in adolescents.

Acne vulgaris is the commonest skin disease in adolescents, affecting 80-90% of teenagers, with significant psychological impact (depression, anxiety, low self-esteem, social withdrawal). Pathogenesis: follicular hyperkeratinisation, sebum production, Cutibacterium acnes, inflammation. Comedonal, inflammatory, and nodulocystic forms. Treatment is severity-based: mild (topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide), moderate (add topical/oral antibiotics - doxycycline, lymecycline), severe (isotretinoin, only under specialist supervision with pregnancy prevention programme). Hormonal therapy (OCP, spironolactone) in females.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Acne in adolescents?

Prevalence: 80-90% of adolescents; peak 14-17 yrs (girls) / 16-19 yrs (boys). Pathogenesis: Follicular hyperkeratinisation, increased sebum, C. acnes, inflammation. Severity grading: Mild (comedonal), moderate (inflammatory), severe (nodulocystic). See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Acne in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Psychological impact - Depression, anxiety, low self-esteem, social withdrawal, suicidality; Mild treatment - Topical retinoid (adapalene) +/- benzoyl peroxide (BPO). See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Acne in adolescents?

Investigations include: Moderate treatment - Add topical/oral antibiotic (doxycycline 100 mg, lymecycline 408 mg, 3 months); Severe treatment - Oral isotretinoin (0.5-1 mg/kg) - 16-24 weeks; cumulative 120-150 mg/kg. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Acne in adolescents?

First-line management: Isotretinoin monitoring - LFTs, lipids, mood; pregnancy prevention (PPP - iPLEDGE in US); Hormonal therapy - Combined OCP (with cyproterone/levonorgestrel), spironolactone (off-label). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Acne in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Scarring - Treat early; isotretinoin for scarring acne regardless of severity. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

  • Mnemonic for Acne in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Anticipatory Guidance And Health Promotion
topics.html:
Definition

Anticipatory guidance is proactive counselling on health, development, and safety to prevent problems before they occur. For adolescents, it covers the HEEADSSS domains, plus immunisation, nutrition, physical activity, sleep, screen time, sexual health, mental health, substance use, and injury prevention. The Bright Futures (AAP) and Youre Welcome (UK DoH) frameworks set quality standards for adolescent-friendly health services. Effective delivery is brief, repeated, and embedded in routine visits. The 5 Rs (Relevance, Risk, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition) enhance retention.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Proactive counselling to prevent problems before they arise

Domains

HEEADSSS + immunisation, nutrition, activity, sleep, screen, safety

Bright Futures (AAP)

US framework for adolescent preventive care

Youre Welcome (UK DoH)

Quality standards for adolescent-friendly health services

5 Rs of anticipatory guidance

Relevance, Risk, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition

Immunisations

HPV (12-13), Td/IPV booster (14), MenACWY (14), MMR catch-up, flu

Sleep

8-10 hrs/night; avoid screens 1 hr before bed; consistent schedule

Screen time

<2 hrs recreational; screen-free meals; bedroom screen-free

Physical activity

=60 min moderate-vigorous daily; muscle-strengthening 3x/week

Injury prevention

Seatbelts, helmets, no driving under influence, drowning prevention

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionProactive counselling to prevent problems before they arise
DomainsHEEADSSS + immunisation, nutrition, activity, sleep, screen, safety
Bright Futures (AAP)US framework for adolescent preventive care

5 Rs of anticipatory guidanceRelevance, Risk, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition

Physical activity>=60 min moderate-vigorous daily; muscle-strengthening 3x/week
Injury preventionSeatbelts, helmets, no driving under influence, drowning prevention

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Anticipatory guidance and health promotion.

Anticipatory guidance is proactive counselling on health, development, and safety to prevent problems before they occur. For adolescents, it covers the HEEADSSS domains, plus immunisation, nutrition, physical activity, sleep, screen time, sexual health, mental health, substance use, and injury prevention. The Bright Futures (AAP) and Youre Welcome (UK DoH) frameworks set quality standards for adolescent-friendly health services. Effective delivery is brief, repeated, and embedded in routine visits. The 5 Rs (Relevance, Risk, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition) enhance retention.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Anticipatory guidance and health promotion?

Definition: Proactive counselling to prevent problems before they arise. Domains: HEEADSSS + immunisation, nutrition, activity, sleep, screen, safety. Bright Futures (AAP): US framework for adolescent preventive care. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Anticipatory guidance and health promotion recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Youre Welcome (UK DoH) - Quality standards for adolescent-friendly health services; 5 Rs of anticipatory guidance - Relevance, Risk, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Anticipatory guidance and health promotion?

Investigations include: Immunisations - HPV (12-13), Td/IPV booster (14), MenACWY (14), MMR catch-up, flu; Sleep - 8-10 hrs/night; avoid screens 1 hr before bed; consistent schedule. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Anticipatory guidance and health promotion?

First-line management: Screen time - <2 hrs recreational; screen-free meals; bedroom screen-free; Physical activity - >=60 min moderate-vigorous daily; muscle-strengthening 3x/week. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Anticipatory guidance and health promotion?

Red flags and complications: Injury prevention - Seatbelts, helmets, no driving under influence, drowning prevention. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Anticipatory guidance and health promotion - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Obesity In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescent obesity (BMI >95th centile for age/sex; overweight 85-95th) affects 20-25% of UK adolescents and is rising globally, with major consequences for current and adult health. Causes: energy imbalance, food environment, sedentary behaviour, sleep deprivation, mental health, endocrine (rare - Cushing, hypothyroid, Prader-Willi). Complications: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, NAFLD, PCOS, obstructive sleep apnoea, musculoskeletal, depression, low self-esteem. Management: family-based multi-component intervention (diet, physical activity, behaviour change), pharmacotherapy (orlistat >12 yrs; semaglutide emerging), bariatric surgery (severe cases).

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence (UK)

20-25% overweight or obese; higher in deprived areas

Definition

BMI >95th centile = obese; 85-95th = overweight (2-18 yrs)

Causes

Energy imbalance, food environment, sedentary behaviour, sleep deprivation, mental health

Endocrine causes

Cushing, hypothyroid, Prader-Willi, growth hormone deficiency (rare)

Complications

T2DM, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, NAFLD, PCOS, OSA, MSK pain, depression

Assessment

BMI, BP, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, LFTs, TFTs; consider OGTT

First-line management

Family-based multi-component: diet, physical activity, behaviour change

Pharmacotherapy

Orlistat >12 yrs; semaglutide emerging; consider in BMI >95th + comorbidity

Q1. Define Obesity in adolescents.

Adolescent obesity (BMI >95th centile for age/sex; overweight 85-95th) affects 20-25% of UK adolescents and is rising globally, with major consequences for current and adult health. topics.title: Anxiety Disorders In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Anxiety disorders (generalised anxiety, panic, social anxiety, separation anxiety, specific phobias) are the commonest mental health disorders in adolescents with prevalence ~10-20%. They often co-occur with depression, OCD, eating disorders and substance use. Presentation may be somatic (headache, abdominal pain, fatigue, school refusal). Screening (GAD-7, SCARED) helps identify. Treatment: psychoeducation, parent training, CBT (first-line) including exposure therapy, SSRIs for moderate-severe. School avoidance/refusal is a common presentation and may require multidisciplinary input (school, mental health, family).

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

10-20% of adolescents; commonest mental health disorder

Common types

GAD, social anxiety, separation anxiety, specific phobias, panic, OCD-related

Somatic presentation

Headache, abdominal pain, fatigue, palpitations - medical workup may be normal

School refusal

Common presenting feature; often driven by social anxiety or bullying

Comorbidity

Depression, OCD, eating disorders, ADHD, substance use very common

Screening

GAD-7, SCARED (Screen for Child Anxiety Related Disorders)

First-line therapy

CBT (including exposure) - 8-12 sessions; parent involvement key

SSRIs

Fluoxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine for moderate-severe; allow 4-6 weeks

School-based interventions

Psychoeducation, exam adjustments, gradual return, mentor support

Prognosis

Half remit by adulthood; comorbidity worsens outcome

Q1. Define Anxiety disorders in adolescents.

Anxiety disorders (generalised anxiety, panic, social anxiety, separation anxiety, specific phobias) are the commonest mental health disorders in adolescents with prevalence ~10-20%. They often co-occur with depression, OCD, eating disorders and substance use. Presentation may be somatic (headache, abdominal pain, fatigue, school refusal). Screening (GAD-7, SCARED) helps identify. Treatment: psychoeducation, parent training, CBT (first-line) including exposure therapy, SSRIs for moderate-severe. School avoidance/refusal is a common presentation and may require multidisciplinary input (school, mental health, family).

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Anxiety disorders in adolescents?

Prevalence: 10-20% of adolescents; commonest mental health disorder. Common types: GAD, social anxiety, separation anxiety, specific phobias, panic, OCD-related. Somatic presentation: Headache, abdominal pain, fatigue, palpitations - medical workup may be normal. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Anxiety disorders in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: School refusal - Common presenting feature; often driven by social anxiety or bullying; Comorbidity - Depression, OCD, eating disorders, ADHD, substance use very common. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Anxiety disorders in adolescents?

Investigations include: Screening - GAD-7, SCARED (Screen for Child Anxiety Related Disorders); First-line therapy - CBT (including exposure) - 8-12 sessions; parent involvement key. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

First-line management: SSRIs - Fluoxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine for moderate-severe; allow 4-6 weeks; School-based interventions - Psychoeducation, exam adjustments, gradual return, mentor support. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Anxiety disorders in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - Half remit by adulthood; comorbidity worsens outcome. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

topics.title: Depression In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Adolescent depression is a mood disorder with prevalence ~5% in mid-adolescence, rising with age, and a major risk factor for suicide. Presentation differs from adults - irritability is more common than sadness, somatisation, behavioural problems, school decline, social withdrawal. Diagnosis uses DSM-5 criteria adapted for adolescents (irritability counts; weight changes may be growth-failure). Screening (PHQ-A, MFQ) is recommended in primary care. Treatment is multimodal: psychoeducation, family therapy, CBT or interpersonal therapy for mild-moderate; SSRI (fluoxetine first-line) for moderate-severe. Antidepressants carry an FDA black-box warning for suicidality - monitor closely.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

2-8% of adolescents; F > M after puberty

Presentation

Irritability more than sadness; somatisation, boredom, school decline, substance use

DSM-5 criteria

=5 of 9 symptoms for >=2 weeks; must include low mood or anhedonia

Duration

Symptoms most of day, nearly every day, >=2 weeks

Screening tools

PHQ-A, MFQ (Mood and Feelings Questionnaire) - high sensitivity

Risk factors

Family history, prior episodes, ACEs, chronic illness, bullying, gender identity issues

First-line therapy

CBT or interpersonal therapy (IPT) - 6-12 sessions; group format effective

SSRI

Fluoxetine first-line (FDA-approved >=8 yrs); sertraline, citalopram alternatives

FDA black box

Suicidality increased in first weeks; weekly monitoring for 4 weeks

TCAs

Avoid in adolescents - cardiotoxicity in overdose

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Prevalence2-8% of adolescents; F > M after puberty
PresentationIrritability more than sadness; somatisation, boredom, school decline, substance use
DSM-5 criteria>=5 of 9 symptoms for >=2 weeks; must include low mood or anhedonia

FDA black boxSuicidality increased in first weeks; weekly monitoring for 4 weeks
TCAsAvoid in adolescents - cardiotoxicity in overdose

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Depression in adolescents.

Adolescent depression is a mood disorder with prevalence ~5% in mid-adolescence, rising with age, and a major risk factor for suicide. Presentation differs from adults - irritability is more common than sadness, somatisation, behavioural problems, school decline, social withdrawal. Diagnosis uses DSM-5 criteria adapted for adolescents (irritability counts; weight changes may be growth-failure). Screening (PHQ-A, MFQ) is recommended in primary care. Treatment is multimodal: psychoeducation, family therapy, CBT or interpersonal therapy for mild-moderate; SSRI (fluoxetine first-line) for moderate-severe. Antidepressants carry an FDA black-box warning for suicidality - monitor closely.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Depression in adolescents?

Prevalence: 2-8% of adolescents; F > M after puberty. Presentation: Irritability more than sadness; somatisation, boredom, school decline, substance use. DSM-5 criteria: >=5 of 9 symptoms for >=2 weeks; must include low mood or anhedonia. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Depression in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Duration - Symptoms most of day, nearly every day, >=2 weeks; Screening tools - PHQ-A, MFQ (Mood and Feelings Questionnaire) - high sensitivity. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Depression in adolescents?

Investigations include: Risk factors - Family history, prior episodes, ACEs, chronic illness, bullying, gender identity issues; First-line therapy - CBT or interpersonal therapy (IPT) - 6-12 sessions; group format effective. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Depression in adolescents?

First-line management: SSRI - Fluoxetine first-line (FDA-approved >=8 yrs); sertraline, citalopram alternatives; FDA black box - Suicidality increased in first weeks; weekly monitoring for 4 weeks. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Depression in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: TCAs - Avoid in adolescents - cardiotoxicity in overdose. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Depression in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Eating Disorders Anorexia Nervosa
topics.html:
Definition

Anorexia nervosa (AN) is a restrictive eating disorder with deliberate weight loss, intense fear of gaining weight, and disturbance in self-perceived body shape (DSM-5). Two subtypes: restricting and binge-purge. Diagnostic threshold: significantly low weight (BMI <18.5 in adults; <5th centile in adolescents). Adolescent onset is typical; F:M ratio 10:1. Medical complications: bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, amenorrhoea, osteoporosis, electrolyte disturbance. Management: medical stabilisation, family-based therapy (FBT/Maudsley) first-line for adolescents, CBT-E, nutritional rehabilitation, SSRI (fluoxetine) for comorbid OCD/depression. Inpatient care for medical instability.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Restriction -> low weight; intense fear of gaining weight; body image disturbance

Subtypes

Restricting; Binge-eating/purging (compensatory behaviours)

BMI threshold

Adult <18.5; adolescent <5th centile (less reliable than weight history)

Prevalence

0.5-1% F; M:F = 1:10; peak onset 13-18 yrs

Risk factors

Perfectionism, family history, low self-esteem, ballet/athletics, dieting, ACEs

Medical complications

Bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, amenorrhoea, osteoporosis, lanugo, electrolytes

Refeeding syndrome

Hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia; start at low kcal, monitor

Family-based therapy (FBT)

Maudsley model; first-line for adolescents; 3 phases (weight restoration -> control -> adolescent)

Medical admission criteria

BMI <13, HR <40, BP <80/50, electrolytes, temperature <35, syncope

Prognosis

50% recover, 25% chronic, 5-10% mortality (highest of psychiatric disorders)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteriaRestriction -> low weight; intense fear of gaining weight; body image disturbance
SubtypesRestricting; Binge-eating/purging (compensatory behaviours)
BMI thresholdAdult <18.5; adolescent <5th centile (less reliable than weight history)

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa.

Anorexia nervosa (AN) is a restrictive eating disorder with deliberate weight loss, intense fear of gaining weight, and disturbance in self-perceived body shape (DSM-5). Two subtypes: restricting and binge-purge. Diagnostic threshold: significantly low weight (BMI <18.5 in adults; <5th centile in adolescents). Adolescent onset is typical; F:M ratio 10:1. Medical complications: bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, amenorrhoea, osteoporosis, electrolyte disturbance. Management: medical stabilisation, family-based therapy (FBT/Maudsley) first-line for adolescents, CBT-E, nutritional rehabilitation, SSRI (fluoxetine) for comorbid OCD/depression. Inpatient care for medical instability.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa?

DSM-5 criteria: Restriction -> low weight; intense fear of gaining weight; body image disturbance. Subtypes: Restricting; Binge-eating/purging (compensatory behaviours). BMI threshold: Adult <18.5; adolescent <5th centile (less reliable than weight history). See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Prevalence - 0.5-1% F; M:F = 1:10; peak onset 13-18 yrs; Risk factors - Perfectionism, family history, low self-esteem, ballet/athletics, dieting, ACEs. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa?

Investigations include: Medical complications - Bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, amenorrhoea, osteoporosis, lanugo, electrolytes; Refeeding syndrome - Hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia; start at low kcal, monitor. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa?

First-line management: Family-based therapy (FBT) - Maudsley model; first-line for adolescents; 3 phases (weight restoration -> control -> adolescent); Medical admission criteria - BMI <13, HR <40, BP <80/50, electrolytes, temperature <35, syncope. Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - 50% recover, 25% chronic, 5-10% mortality (highest of psychiatric disorders). Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Eating disorders - anorexia nervosa - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Eating Disorders Bulimia Nervosa And Binge Eating
topics.html:
Definition

Bulimia nervosa (BN) is a recurrent binge-eating disorder with compensatory behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, exercise, fasting) at least weekly for 3 months, in normal-weight or overweight individuals. Binge eating disorder (BED) is recurrent binge eating without compensation. Both peak in late adolescence/early adulthood; F:M ratio lower than AN (3:1 for BN). Complications of BN: electrolyte disturbance (hypokalaemia), dental erosions, parotid hypertrophy, oesophagitis/gastritis, Russell sign (knuckle calluses). Management: CBT-E (enhanced CBT) first-line; fluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed medication for BN); addressing underlying mood/anxiety.

Core Clinical Content

BN DSM-5

Recurrent binges + compensatory behaviours, >=weekly, >=3 months, normal weight

BED DSM-5

Recurrent binges without compensation; marked distress

Compensatory behaviours

Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, exercise, fasting

Physical signs

Russell sign (knuckle calluses), parotid hypertrophy, dental erosions, eroded enamel

Electrolytes

Hypokalaemia, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis, dehydration

Complications

Oesophagitis, gastric dilatation, arrhythmias, sudden death (hypokalaemia)

Differential AN binge-purge

AN with binge-purge: low weight; BN: normal or overweight

CBT-E

Enhanced cognitive behavioural therapy - first-line for BN and BED

Pharmacotherapy BN

Fluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed drug for adult BN)

Prognosis

Better than AN; 50-70% recover; risk of relapse

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
BN DSM-5Recurrent binges + compensatory behaviours, >=weekly, >=3 months, normal weight
BED DSM-5Recurrent binges without compensation; marked distress
Compensatory behavioursSelf-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, exercise, fasting
Physical signsRussell sign (knuckle calluses), parotid hypertrophy, dental erosions, eroded enamel
ElectrolytesHypokalaemia, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis, dehydration
ComplicationsOesophagitis, gastric dilatation, arrhythmias, sudden death (hypokalaemia)
Differential AN binge-purgeAN with binge-purge: low weight; BN: normal or overweight
CBT-EEnhanced cognitive behavioural therapy - first-line for BN and BED
Pharmacotherapy BNFluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed drug for adult BN)
PrognosisBetter than AN; 50-70% recover; risk of relapse

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating.

Bulimia nervosa (BN) is a recurrent binge-eating disorder with compensatory behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, exercise, fasting) at least weekly for 3 months, in normal-weight or overweight individuals. Binge eating disorder (BED) is recurrent binge eating without compensation. Both peak in late adolescence/early adulthood; F:M ratio lower than AN (3:1 for BN). Complications of BN: electrolyte disturbance (hypokalaemia), dental erosions, parotid hypertrophy, oesophagitis/gastritis, Russell sign (knuckle calluses). Management: CBT-E (enhanced CBT) first-line; fluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed medication for BN); addressing underlying mood/anxiety.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating?

BN DSM-5: Recurrent binges + compensatory behaviours, >=weekly, >=3 months, normal weight. BED DSM-5: Recurrent binges without compensation; marked distress. Compensatory behaviours: Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, exercise, fasting. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Physical signs - Russell sign (knuckle calluses), parotid hypertrophy, dental erosions, eroded enamel; Electrolytes - Hypokalaemia, hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis, dehydration. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating?

Investigations include: Complications - Oesophagitis, gastric dilatation, arrhythmias, sudden death (hypokalaemia); Differential AN binge-purge - AN with binge-purge: low weight; BN: normal or overweight. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating?

First-line management: CBT-E - Enhanced cognitive behavioural therapy - first-line for BN and BED; Pharmacotherapy BN - Fluoxetine 60 mg (only licensed drug for adult BN). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating?

Red flags and complications: Prognosis - Better than AN; 50-70% recover; risk of relapse. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

```
Eating disorders - bulimia nervosa and binge eating

├── Definition & Core Concept

├── Clinical Recognition

│ ├── Presentation

│ └── Examination

├── Investigation

├── Management

│ ├── Acute

│ └── Long-term

└── Complications

├── Short-term

└── Long-term

```

topics.title: Self Harm And Suicide In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-poisoning or injury without suicidal intent; suicide is self-harm with intent to die. Both are leading causes of adolescent death globally - suicide is the 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 year-olds. Risk factors: prior self-harm, mental illness (depression, BPD), substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT identity, easy access to means. Immediate management: medical stabilisation of injury, mental health assessment, safety planning, restricting means (especially ligature points, medications, firearms). Long-term: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, CBT, family therapy, school-based prevention.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Suicide 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 yrs; F > M attempts, M > F deaths

NSSI prevalence

10-20% of adolescents; cutting, burning, hitting, scratching

Risk factors

Prior self-harm, mental illness, substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT, access to means

Warning signs

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Self-harm and suicide in adolescents.

Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-poisoning or injury without suicidal intent; suicide is self-harm with intent to die. Both are leading causes of adolescent death globally - suicide is the 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 year-olds. Risk factors: prior self-harm, mental illness (depression, BPD), substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT identity, easy access to means. Immediate management: medical stabilisation of injury, mental health assessment, safety planning, restricting means (especially ligature points, medications, firearms). Long-term: dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, CBT, family therapy, school-based prevention.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

Epidemiology: Suicide 2nd-3rd leading cause of death in 15-24 yrs; F > M attempts, M > F deaths. NSSI prevalence: 10-20% of adolescents; cutting, burning, hitting, scratching. Risk factors: Prior self-harm, mental illness, substance use, ACEs, bullying, LGBT, access to means. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Self-harm and suicide in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Warning signs - Giving possessions, saying goodbye, sudden calmness, talking about death; Means restriction - Remove access: ligature points, medications, firearms, pesticides. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

Investigations include: Immediate management - ABCDE medical stabilisation; mental state exam; suicide risk assessment; Risk assessment - Intent, plan, means, timeline, prior attempts, protective factors. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

First-line management: Safety plan - Written; identifies warning signs, coping, contacts, restricted means, ED if needed; Long-term therapy - DBT for BPD; CBT for depression/anxiety; safety planning intervention (SPI). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Self-harm and suicide in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Media reporting - Avoid detail on method; do not glorify; encourage help-seeking (Papageno effect). Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

  • Mnemonic for Self-harm and suicide in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Bipolar Disorder In Adolescents
topics.html:
Definition

Bipolar disorder in adolescents is characterised by episodes of elevated/irritable mood with increased energy/activity (mania/hypomania) alternating with periods of depression. DSM-5 distinguishes bipolar I (>=1 manic episode, duration >=7 days or hospitalisation) and bipolar II (>=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode). Mixed episodes and rapid cycling are common in adolescents. Prevalence 1-3% adolescents; M=F; high suicide risk (15-20x general population). Often misdiagnosed as ADHD, conduct disorder, or unipolar depression. Management: mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine), atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone), psychotherapy (CBT, family-focused, IPSRT). Comorbid ADHD/anxiety common. Prognosis: chronic relapsing course; treatment adherence a major challenge.

Core Clinical Content

Bipolar I

=1 manic episode (>=7 days or hospitalisation)

Bipolar II

=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode

Mania criteria (DIG FAST)

Distractibility, Irresponsibility, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increased, Sleep decreased, Talkativeness

Prevalence

1-3% adolescents; M=F

Suicide risk

15-20x general population; highest in mixed episodes

Misdiagnosis

Often confused with ADHD, conduct disorder, unipolar depression

First-line pharmacotherapy

Lithium (gold standard), valproate, atypical antipsychotics

Lithium monitoring

TFTs, U&E, Ca, pregnancy test; narrow therapeutic index

Lamotrigine

Effective for bipolar depression; slow titration (Stevens-Johnson risk)

Valproate

Avoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Bipolar I>=1 manic episode (>=7 days or hospitalisation)
Bipolar II>=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode

MisdiagnosisOften confused with ADHD, conduct disorder, unipolar depression

LamotrigineEffective for bipolar depression; slow titration (Stevens-Johnson risk)
ValproateAvoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania

Viva Questions

Q1. Define Bipolar disorder in adolescents.

Bipolar disorder in adolescents is characterised by episodes of elevated/irritable mood with increased energy/activity (mania/hypomania) alternating with periods of depression. DSM-5 distinguishes bipolar I (>=1 manic episode, duration >=7 days or hospitalisation) and bipolar II (>=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode). Mixed episodes and rapid cycling are common in adolescents. Prevalence 1-3% adolescents; M=F; high suicide risk (15-20x general population). Often misdiagnosed as ADHD, conduct disorder, or unipolar depression. Management: mood stabilisers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine), atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone), psychotherapy (CBT, family-focused, IPSRT). Comorbid ADHD/anxiety common. Prognosis: chronic relapsing course; treatment adherence a major challenge.

Q2. What are the most important FCPS/MRCP high-yield points for Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

Bipolar I: >=1 manic episode (>=7 days or hospitalisation). Bipolar II: >=1 hypomanic + >=1 major depressive episode. Mania criteria (DIG FAST): Distractibility, Irresponsibility, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increased, Sleep decreased, Talkativeness. See FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table below.

Q3. How is Bipolar disorder in adolescents recognised clinically?

Presentation and key clinical features include: Prevalence - 1-3% adolescents; M=F; Suicide risk - 15-20x general population; highest in mixed episodes. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q4. What investigations are required for Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

Investigations include: Misdiagnosis - Often confused with ADHD, conduct disorder, unipolar depression; First-line pharmacotherapy - Lithium (gold standard), valproate, atypical antipsychotics. See Core Clinical Content for full details.

Q5. What is the first-line management of Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

First-line management: Lithium monitoring - TFTs, U&E, Ca, pregnancy test; narrow therapeutic index; Lamotrigine - Effective for bipolar depression; slow titration (Stevens-Johnson risk). Refer to specialist adolescent services for definitive management where indicated.

Q6. What are the key complications or red flags of Bipolar disorder in adolescents?

Red flags and complications: Valproate - Avoid in females (teratogenicity); useful in males, severe mania. Always consider safeguarding, mental health comorbidity, and transition planning in adolescent presentations.

  • Mnemonic for Bipolar disorder in adolescents - see the FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary table above for the 10 high-yield facts
  • 'HEEADSSS' assessment - Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety (3.0: + Eating, Sleep, Screen) - always use in adolescent history
  • 'Gillick competence' - under-16 with sufficient understanding to make own medical decisions
  • 'Fraser guidelines' - specific criteria for contraceptive/sexual health advice without parental knowledge
  • 'HEADSS' / 'SSHADESS' - alternative psychosocial assessment frameworks (Strengths-based)

topics.title: Conduct Disorder And Oppositional Defiant Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Conduct disorder (CD) is a persistent pattern of behaviour in adolescents that violates the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms, including aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, and serious rule violations. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting >=6 months, but without the serious violations of CD. CD affects 3-5% of adolescents, M:F 3:1, often comorbid with ADHD, depression, substance use. Management: multimodal — parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills training, school intervention; severe cases may need residential or forensic placement. Prognosis improves with early intervention; ~50% desist by adulthood.

Core Clinical Content

Conduct disorder DSM-5

=3 criteria in 15 months from 4 groups: aggression, property, deceit, rules

ODD DSM-5

Angry/irritable, argumentative/defiant, vindictive >=6 months

Prevalence

CD 3-5%; ODD 2-16%; M:F 3:1

Comorbidity

ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disability

Risk factors

Genetics, harsh parenting, ACEs, poverty, school failure

Severity specifiers

Mild, moderate, severe; age of onset (before/after 10 yrs)

First-line therapy

Parent management training (PMT), CBT, social skills, school intervention

Pharmacotherapy

Risperidone for severe aggression; stimulants for comorbid ADHD

Prognosis

50% desist by adulthood; worse with early onset, comorbidity, criminality

Differential

ODD, ADHD, bipolar, ASD, depression, substance use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
